questions.scenario:
The pipeline pushed this image:

111122223333.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/devpolaris-orders-api:2026-05-02.7

But ECS service details show: Service taskDefinition is devpolaris-orders-api:41. Task definition 41 image is ...:2026-05-02.6. RunningCount is 2.

What is missing from the release path?

options.text: Register a new task definition revision but leave the service on revision 41 until the mutable image tag refreshes.
Create a second service for the same production workload so the new image can run beside the old task definition.
A new task definition revision that points at the new image, followed by a service update to that revision.
Force a deployment without registering a revision because the service will resolve a mutable image tag at each health check.

questions.explanation:
An ECR image is passive until the runtime recipe and service use it. The task definition revision is the bridge from artifact to running tasks. Updating the service is what starts the deployment path.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS services](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ecs_services.html).

questions.scenario:
A new Fargate task reaches RUNNING, but the ALB cannot complete its health check. The target and application evidence is:

```text
target=10.0.42.18:3000 state=unhealthy reason=Target.Timeout
health_check=GET /health on traffic port
app_log=server listening address=127.0.0.1 port=3000
```

Which change should the team make before sending production traffic to the task?

options.text: Open task inbound port 3000 from the ALB security group but keep the process bound to `127.0.0.1`.
Increase the health-check timeout because `Target.Timeout` can indicate a slow but reachable readiness endpoint.
Change the target-group port to the container's loopback listener instead of changing the application bind address.
Change the app to listen on 0.0.0.0 inside the container so the task ENI can receive ALB traffic.

questions.explanation:
The app is listening only on loopback, so the ALB cannot reach it through the task ENI. Fargate with awsvpc commonly uses IP targets. The public listener can stay on 443 while the private target group forwards to port 3000.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer target health](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/target-group-health-checks.html).

questions.scenario:
A stopped task for devpolaris-orders-api shows:

```text
CannotPullContainerError: failed to resolve ref
111122223333.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/devpolaris-orders-api:2026-05-02.10: not found
```

What should be inspected first?

options.text: The task definition image reference and whether that tag or digest exists in the expected ECR repository and Region.
Force another deployment of the same task definition so ECR has time to create the missing tag.
Add broader ECR permissions to the application task role, even though image pulls use the task execution path.
Change the target-group health path so ECS can start the container before resolving its image.

questions.explanation:
The task never reached application startup because the configured image reference could not be resolved. The first check is the exact repository, Region, tag or digest, and task-definition revision. Repeating the same revision does not create the tag, the application task role is not the image-pull identity, and health configuration is evaluated only after a container starts.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS task definitions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_definitions.html).

questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris platform team is defining the conditions a replacement ECS task must satisfy before it receives production traffic for devpolaris-orders-api should include:

Public URL is https://orders.devpolaris.com. ALB listener is HTTPS 443. Target group is HTTP 3000, target type ip. Task security group is inbound 3000 from ALB security group. Node.js log is listening on 0.0.0.0:3000.

Which entries are part of that contract?

options.text: The task security group should allow the ALB security group on the app port.
Each Fargate task should expose port 3000 directly to the public internet.
The target group should register Fargate task IPs on the container port.
The Node.js process should listen on the same port the target group checks.
Confirm the service deployment is complete and infer that each registered target passed health evaluation.

questions.explanation:
The ALB is the public front door, and the tasks stay private. The task IP, security group, container port, and app listener must line up. Exposing each task directly would bypass the load balancer model.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario:
During an ECS rollout, the service reports two RUNNING tasks but customers receive 503 responses. Both registered targets show the same result:

```text
10.0.42.18:3000  unhealthy  Target.Timeout
10.0.43.27:3000  unhealthy  Target.Timeout
```

A teammate argues that RUNNING proves the tasks can receive ALB traffic. How should the release captain respond?

options.text: Treat the timeout as a security-group failure without checking whether the app binds the task interface or responds before the timeout.
Running tasks are not enough. Target health shows whether the ALB can reach and accept each target's health response.
Increase the health-check timeout before confirming whether the ALB can establish the connection.
Change the health path first even though an incorrect reachable path would usually return an HTTP mismatch.

questions.explanation:
ECS running state and ALB target health answer different questions. A process can exist while the ALB cannot reach it, the port is wrong, or the health endpoint times out. Target health is the evidence for the load balancer's view.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer target health](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/target-group-health-checks.html).

questions.scenario:
Right after a deployment, target health shows:

```text
10.0.42.18  3000  healthy
10.0.43.27  3000  initial  Elb.InitialHealthChecking
```

What should you conclude?

options.text: Extend the health-check interval because `initial` means the target has already failed its first probe.
Deregister and register the second target again because `initial` means ECS registered the wrong task address.
Increase desired count because a task in `initial` indicates the service lacks enough registered targets.
One target is ready, and the other may simply be new while initial health checks run.

questions.explanation:
`initial` is normal for a newly registered target while the ALB checks it. It is not automatically an outage. Watch whether it becomes healthy or moves to an unhealthy reason.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer target health](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/target-group-health-checks.html).

questions.scenario:
A replacement task is repeatedly stopped before its application finishes booting. ECS events and the final buffered application log establish this order:

```text
10:41:18 target 10.0.44.92:3000 registered
10:41:48 task failed load balancer health checks
10:41:49 task stopped
10:41:52 buffered log received: HTTP server listening on 0.0.0.0:3000
```

Which interpretation best explains the failed rollout?

options.text: The app starts listening after ECS has already treated the target as failed, so startup timing and grace settings need review.
Inspect task security groups and port binding because a timeout means the probe did not receive an HTTP response.
Inspect the health-check path first because an invalid route can produce a response-code mismatch rather than a timeout.
Increase the health-check grace period without measuring startup time or checking whether the late listener is expected.

questions.explanation:
The evidence shows a timing problem: health checks fail before the server is ready. Possible fixes include reducing slow startup work, starting the HTTP listener earlier with accurate readiness, or tuning the ECS health check grace period. Always returning `200` would hide the readiness bug.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer target health](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/target-group-health-checks.html).
